title: Work with a Single Purpose! Victory Is Ours!
translatorNote: This translation is intended to carry Kalaignar's voice into clear, contemporary English rather than recast the letter as literary or academic prose. It preserves the source's argument, political directness, rhetorical questions, repetition, irony, factual detail, and paragraph order. Names, dates, figures, quotations, and intentional English expressions are retained. Where Tamil idiom cannot be reproduced literally without sounding unnatural, the English follows its sense and rhetorical force without adding claims absent from the source. The original Tamil is reproduced in full below the translation and remains the authoritative text. `Udanpirappē` is retained in Tamil transliteration rather than flattened into “brother,” “sister,” or “comrade.” Literally evoking “one born alongside me,” Kalaignar uses it as a distinctive address of shared identity, equality, affection, and solidarity within the movement. Seat allocations, candidate counts and qualifications are retained exactly as stated. Anna's English sentence is preserved in its printed wording and capitalization, while his 1960 speech is translated as a direct quotation. The Tamil reading copy below repairs only unambiguous OCR corruption and broken word joins. The canonical Tamil source remains unchanged.
paragraphs: The Fifteenth General Election will be held on 16 May. In the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam alliance, forty-one constituencies have been allocated to the Indian National Congress, five to the Indian Union Muslim League, five to the Manithaneya Makkal Katchi, four to Puthiya Tamilagam, and three to the Makkal DMDK. The Perunthalaivar Makkal Katchi, Tamil Nadu Farmers and Workers Party, and Social Equality Force have each been allotted one seat. Thus, sixty-one constituencies have been given to allied parties, and the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam is contesting 173.

Even within the forty-one seats allocated to the Congress, although Arcot had already been announced for that party, the two parties subsequently discussed and agreed that the Congress would contest Sholinghur, which had been allotted to the Kazhagam, and the Kazhagam would contest Arcot.

Yesterday, 13 April 2016, the names of the 173 candidates contesting on behalf of the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam were announced. As the selected Kazhagam candidates meet me happily and drape shawls around me, one pain keeps burrowing into my heart: how can I face in person all those who submitted applications for the same constituencies, attended the review, and now grieve because they did not receive the opportunity?

Some are people I know extremely well, people who have laboured devotedly for the Kazhagam over many years, and people who participated in its struggles and suffered in prison. I wanted to give opportunities to some of them and to their beloved heirs. But the sound view was put to me that certain others possessed a greater chance of victory and that, in this election, it was better to give opportunities to those with the greatest chance of winning than merely to those we personally preferred. Consequently, I could not offer the opportunity to some whom I wished to select. My greatest pain is that they will all be disappointed. I considered mentioning the names of some, but if I named even one person, it would create the impression that I had failed to mention the others.

Of the 173 constituencies contested by the Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam, ninety-seven opportunities have gone to familiar faces and seventy-six to new faces. Nineteen women have been given the opportunity to contest. Of the 173 Kazhagam candidates, 106 are graduates: nine doctors, twenty-seven lawyers, twelve engineers, nineteen postgraduates, three holders of doctoral degrees, and thirty-six undergraduates.

All these candidates submitted applications and attended interviews. From 22 February to 7 March, I participated in the interviews along with General Secretary Professor, Treasurer Stalin, Principal Secretary Duraimurugan, and Organisation Secretary Alandur R.S. Bharathi. We interviewed about five thousand people and spent the past several weeks examining their qualifications, their prospects of victory, and the circumstances. The names now announced are those selected through that process.

I have never doubted that everyone who submitted an application has served the Kazhagam, worked for it in some manner, and, in many cases, participated in its struggles and gone to prison. But as I have already said, the Kazhagam can select only one person in a constituency. Several people may be qualified to contest there on its behalf, yet one among them may have a comparatively greater prospect of victory.

I have already explained how difficult it is to select such a person while remaining “like the balance that weighs evenly after bringing things into equilibrium.” In some constituencies, we had identified winning candidates and were waiting to announce them, but then had to allocate those constituencies to allied parties. In constituencies where several people applied, although we may have regarded many of them as winning candidates, we could announce only one.

Those not announced must not be disappointed. In every constituency, work with the firm conviction that “it is the DMK that is contesting.” Labour day and night with the feeling that I am contesting on your behalf so that I may continue working for you in every constituency; that our symbol is the Rising Sun; and that the symbols of our allied parties are also ours.

A good opportunity will come to everyone. Never forget that success comes only to those who learn to wait until their turn. Anna expressed this beautifully in English: “We should learn to Wait till our Turn comes.” I have pointed this out once before.

My appeal to those who submitted applications is this: this election must result in our Kazhagam winning a majority of seats and forming the Government for the welfare of Tamil Nadu's people. Those who applied to the Kazhagam must not think only that they personally should win. They must hold the unshakable conviction that the DMK must win and that the Rising Sun—the symbol Anna envisioned, gave us and led to victory—must triumph.

Those who received the opportunity to contest must not act with the negative motive that, now that they have been selected, they can “deal with” those who applied against them. They must lovingly embrace and unite everyone and labour with their whole breath for the Kazhagam's victory.
As I have recalled before, Perarignar Anna once said:

> “The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam is not merely a political party; it is a social-reform movement. Among all those engaged in it, it is not important who occupies which place, nor when each person assumes responsibility. Every person holding a responsibility forms part of this movement's battle camp. If enemies try to enter, they will be caught and crushed between the closeness that binds us. > > “The Dravida Munnetra Kazhagam is an organisation created so that each person may work from a particular place. If opponents imagine that there are other gradations, inequalities or resentments among us, we can only say that they do not know the history of our growth. The reason we were not born from one mother's womb is simply that one mother's womb could not bear so many children.”

Perarignar Anna spoke those moving words at a meeting on Chennai's seafront on 26 September 1960. Opponents who do not know that we have always preserved them in our memory wait longingly, wondering whether a split will arise among us and whether they can enter between us and drink blood. In the end, those enemies will only be disappointed.

Therefore, even if small differences exist, Kazhagam Udanpirappugal must forget them and remember the saying, “If one looks only for faults, no kinship will remain.” Keep Anna's sari analogy in mind and work with a broad willingness to yield: if one person served as secretary this time, let another assume that responsibility next time.

I caution you that this is how Kazhagam Udanpirappugal must understand and work. If events instead unfold in the spirit of “we will clash whatever happens,” they will harm those who clash and this movement, which is their own home. They will affect the candidates, all of us, and the very future of Tamil Nadu. I ask you never to let slip from your memory that “if unity departs, decline comes to all.”
Protect Duty, Dignity and Discipline. Work with the single purpose of securing victory for the Kazhagam and victory for the Rising Sun! Victory is ours!
With affection,
M.K.
